title: Microsoft HomeStation — Analyst Briefing Notes
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Briefing notes on Microsoft's HomeStation concept — a home-server platform bridging traditional PC with home stereo and TV, built on Xbox hardware with Windows XP 2.0 (codename Longwood) replacing Windows 2000, Terminal Server support to drive Mira tablets and remote controls, and nVidia TV-tuner integration. Freestyle is the HomeStation UI (demoed at CES 2002). Frames Microsoft as a $1.5B hardware player via mice, WebTV, and Xbox — and HomeStation as a stealth path to become a major PC vendor.
importance_rationale: Primary-source contemporaneous notes on Microsoft's living-room / home-server strategy ahead of the public Media Center PC launch (August 2002); captures strategic framing before it was public.
relevance_rationale: Living-room-PC strategy is well-documented historically; notes add texture on Mira/Xbox/Terminal Server architectural choices.
prescience_rationale: Correctly framed Microsoft's multi-hardware push (Xbox + Mira + Media Center convergence) as stealth PC-vendor strategy; this materialized through Media Center PC, Xbox Media Extenders, and later Xbox as a media hub.
